coredata.dc:title: Polyaniline stabilized Silver (I) Oxide nanocubes for sensitive and selective detection of hemoglobin in urine for hematuria evaluation
coredata.dc:creator: Swaminathan, Nandini
Nerthigan, Yowan
Wu, Hui-Fen
coredata.dc:description: Blood in urine or hematuria has been as an indication of a plethora of urological disorders. Some diagnoses of hematuria may give false positive results, especially in the cases of microhematuria. Nanomaterials based biosensors have been emerged as an effective method for hematuria analysis, which could serve as a viable alternative to urine dipstick method. In this report, the Ag2O nanocubes were synthesized by the co-precipitation method and further stabilized with polyaniline (PANI). The bio-complex formed between PANI stabilized Ag2O nanocubes, and heme group of Hemoglobin (Hb) resulted in an increase of absorption intensity of Ag2O nanocubes at 420 nm. This is due to the electronic transition from Heme to Ag+. This also leads to conformational changes in Hb. Ag2O-PANI interactions and stability studies were confirmed by Ultraviolet-Visible spectroscopy (UV–Vis), Fourier-Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR), X-ray diffraction (XRD) and Raman spectroscopy. The morphology of Ag2O nanocubes was confirmed by Transmission electron Microscopy (TEM). The interaction between Ag2O-PANI and Hb was confirmed by UV–Vis spectroscopy. The current assay is very sensitive for hematuria with an extremely low detection limit of 5.35 × 10−13 M from urine.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nanocubes
Silver oxide
Hemoglobin
Polyaniline
Absorption spectroscopy
Hematuria

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: pH stability studies of the Ag2O-PANI nanocubes
Analysis of Hb from whole blood in urine samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ministry of Science and Technology, Taiwan

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We thank the Ministry of Science and Technology ( MOST ) of Taiwan for financial support with the grant number of MOST 107-2113-M-110-011-MY3 is gratefully acknowledged.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The schematic illustration of Hb detection using Ag2O-PANI nanocubes.
UV–Vis absorption spectra of Ag2O nanocubes, Ag2O-PANI nanocubes, and PANI. Insert: near visible light photos of (a) Ag2O nanocubes and (b) Ag2O-PANI nanocubes.
(a) TEM image of Ag2O nanocubes (b) SAED pattern (c) HR-TEM image showing d-spacing.
XRD patterns of Ag2O nanocubes and Ag2O-PANI nanocubes.
FTIR spectra of Ag2O nanocubes, Ag2O-PANI nanocubes and PANI.
Raman Spectra of Ag2O nanocubes, Ag2O-PANI nanocubes and PANI.

(a) UV–Vis Spectra of Ag2O-PANI nanocubes with various concentrations of Hb (0.01 µM, 0.02 µM, 0.03 µM, 0.04 µM, 0.05 µM) (b) Linear calibration plot of absorption intensity at 410 nm of human Hb concentration (0.005 µM, 0.01 µM, 0.02 µM, 0.03 µM, 0.04 µM, and 0.05 µM) using Ag2O-PANI nanocubes.
Influence of various components present in urine and blood for Hb detection using Ag2O-PANI nanocubes.
Detection of Hb from whole blood in a urine sample.

head.ce:title: Polyaniline stabilized Silver (I) Oxide nanocubes for sensitive and selective detection of hemoglobin in urine for hematuria evaluation

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Blood in urine or hematuria has been as an indication of a plethora of urological disorders. Some diagnoses of hematuria may give false positive results, especially in the cases of microhematuria. Nanomaterials based biosensors have been emerged as an effective method for hematuria analysis, which could serve as a viable alternative to urine dipstick method. In this report, the Ag2O nanocubes were synthesized by the co-precipitation method and further stabilized with polyaniline (PANI). The bio-complex formed between PANI stabilized Ag2O nanocubes, and heme group of Hemoglobin (Hb) resulted in an increase of absorption intensity of Ag2O nanocubes at 420 nm. This is due to the electronic transition from Heme to Ag+. This also leads to conformational changes in Hb. Ag2O-PANI interactions and stability studies were confirmed by Ultraviolet-Visible spectroscopy (UV–Vis), Fourier-Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR), X-ray diffraction (XRD) and Raman spectroscopy. The morphology of Ag2O nanocubes was confirmed by Transmission electron Microscopy (TEM). The interaction between Ag2O-PANI and Hb was confirmed by UV–Vis spectroscopy. The current assay is very sensitive for hematuria with an extremely low detection limit of 5.35 × 10−13 M from urine.

ce:section.ce:para: Hematuria is the presence of Red Blood Cells (RBC) in urine originating from kidney or urinary tract [1]. It can be microscopic or gross hematuria where the former leads to severe consequences as it is invisible to the naked eyes. This condition is generally prevalent in children and men [2]. Hematuria can be an indication of a serious medical condition which can be benign to life-threatening. It is one of the leading manifestations for cancers relating to kidney, bladder, upper urothelial tract and prostrate [3]. It is also one of the common symptoms for urinary tract infections, pyelonephritis, kidney stones and IgA nephropathy [4]. Hence, it is mandatory to develop effective and sensitive methods to evaluate such a urological paradigm. Basic evaluation of microscopic hematuria is performed via urine dipstick or urine analysis [5]. Dipstick urine analysis suffers from false positive or false negative results wherein the patient later undergoes conventional and expensive treatments like cystectomy, chemotherapy, urine cytology which are time-consuming and involve complicated analytical processes [3,6–8].

In this study, we report the synthesis of Ag2O nanocubes stabilized with PANI in the emeraldine form for sensitive detection of Hematuria (Fig.1 ). The Ag2O nanocubes prepared by the hydrothermal method were coated with PANI to prohibit agglomeration and enhance stability. The as-prepared material was characterized by UV–Visible absorption spectroscopy, XRD, FTIR, TEM and Raman spectroscopy. The as-synthesized Ag2O-PANI nanocube was used for highly sensitive detection of human Hb with extremely low detection limit. The assay was also performed to detect spiked Hb from human whole blood in human urine samples.

Ammonium Nitrate (NH4NO3), Sodium dodecylbenzene sulfonate (SDBS), human hemoglobin lyophilized, potassium, sodium chloride, fructose, maltose, cysteine, arginine, lysine, ascorbic acid, asparagine, leucine, ferritin, histidine, creatinine, urea, uric acid, glucose and myoglobin were purchased from Sigma Aldrich, Sodium hydroxide (NaOH) from Uniregion Biotech, Silver Nitrate (AgNO3) from Honey well Fluka, Ammonium Persulfate (APS) from BioBasic Ins., Aniline from Baker Analyzed reagent. All chemicals were of analytical grade and were used without any additional purification in all experiments.

The morphological details of the as-prepared material were studied using TEM (Philips, The Netherlands). The UV absorbance of the nanomaterial was recorded on a UV–Vis spectrometry (Thermo Evolution 201, Thermo Scientific, USA) and it was carried out using a standard quartz cuvette having a path length of 1 cm. Material crystallinity and pattern was elucidated by XRD (Philips, Netherlands). The vibrational conformation was studied by a micro-Raman spectroscopy, using setup λ = 633 nm laser (JOBIN-YVON T64000) and FT-IR (Perkin Elmer USA). pH studies were performed using a pH meter (EcoMet P25,Istek Instrument), and centrifugation was performed using the Hettich Mikro 22R (Hettich, USA) centrifuge.

The Ag2O nanocubes were prepared according to the reported method [39]. Stock solutions of 0.4 M NH4NO3, 0.1 M AgNO3, 2 M NaOH and 0.2 M NaOH were prepared. 125 µl NH4NO3, 25 µl of 2 M NaOH and 250 µl of AgNO3 was added to deionized water to make the final solution volume of 5 ml. After stirring the mixture for 10 min at room temperature, 1 ml of 0.2 M NaOH was added. The solution was stirred until the color changes from colorless to brown, indicating the formation of nanocubes. Later, the final solution was centrifuged at 3500 rpm for 3 min, and the pellet was washed twice with ethanol and dried at room temperature. It was later dispersed in deionized water for further experiments.

Ag2O-PANI nanocubes were prepared by a modified protocol [46]. 500 mg of SDBS was introduced into 10 ml of Ag2O nanocube's solution and sonicated for 30 min. 50 µl of aniline was added to the solution, and the pH was set between 2 and 3 and kept in an ice bath for 1 h 3 ml of 0.1 M APS was added dropwise, and the solution was stirred for 10–12 h in an ice bath until the color changes from milky white to green. The as-prepared solution was later centrifuged for 10 min at 7000 rpm and stored for further experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: pH stability studies of the Ag2O-PANI nanocubes
ce:section.ce:para: The pH Stability studies were performed by introducing 150 µl of Ag2O-PANI nanocubes into 3 ml of various pH (2, 4, 6, 8 and 10) solutions and its absorbance was recorded using an UV–Vis spectrometry.

The Ag2O-PANI nanocubes were introduced into solutions of sugars (glucose, fructose, maltose; amino acids), amino acids (histidine, arginine, asparagine, cysteine, leucine, and lysine) and chemical compounds (potassium chloride, sodium chloride, ferritin, urea, uric acid, ascorbic acid and myoglobin). 150 µl of Ag2O-PANI nanocubes was introduced into 3 ml of the above-mentioned salt solutions at a physiological concentration in PBS (pH 7.4). Then, the UV–Vis spectra were recorded. All studies were performed in triplicates.

For Hb detection, 2.5 ml of PBS (pH 7.4), 150 µl of Ag2O-PANI nanocubes and appropriate aliquots of hHb (human hemoglobin) with varied concentrations (0.005 µM to 0.05 µM) were prepared to a final volume of 3 ml. Then, the UV–Vis spectra were recorded.

A fresh drop of finger prick blood sample from a healthy human volunteer was collected in 1 ml of PBS (pH 7.4) using a sterile needle. It was later centrifuged to separate the RBC. The supernatant was discarded, and the fresh PBS was introduced into the pellet. This solution was used as a stock RBC. 150 µl of fresh urine sample (filtered) was added in 1 ml of PBS (pH 7.4). Hematuria studies were performed by introducing varied concentration of RBC into a fixed concentration of Ag2O-PANI nanocubes and urine. The observations were recorded by an UV–Vis absorption spectrometer.

The co-precipitation method was used for the synthesis of Ag2O nanocubes using NH4NO3, NaOH and AgNO3 in a fixed molar ratio. Ag2O nanocubes can be synthesized by direct addition of NaOH into AgNO3 solution. However, this leads to a drastic reduction in the solubility product constant (K = 1/Ksp); hence, the morphology can't be controlled as reported in previous works [47]. The use of NH4NO3 was preferred for the source of ammonia (NH3) over the direct use of NH3 as it makes the solution basic, which leads to uncontrolled shapes and sizes [25]. The aqueous solution of NH4NO3 and NaOH forms NH3 which reacts to AgNO3, forms the complex Ag (NH3)2 + ions. These complex ions control the reaction rate and the growth of nanocubes. 0.2 M of NaOH was added to break these ions into silver hydroxide (AgOH) and NH3.

As formed AgOH further could dissociate into Ag2O and H2O as shown in the following equations.
(1) NH4NO3 + NaOH NH3 + H2O + Na+ + NO3 −
(2) AgNO3 + 2NH3 Ag (NH3)2 + + NO3 −
(3) Ag (NH3)2 + + NaOH AgOH + 2NH3 + Na+
(4) 2AgOH Ag2O + H2O

The as prepared Ag2O nanocubes were further coated by PANI to prevent the agglomeration of nanocubes. It is important to note that, polymerization of aniline was conducted at low temperature and under aqueous acidic conditions, as they are favorable for effective synthesis of emeraldine PANI which is more stable and conductive [46].

The UV–Vis absorption spectroscopy was performed to investigate the optical characteristics of as-prepared Ag2O nanocubes. PANI and PANI coated Ag2O nanocubes dispersed in DI water at pH ~ 6.5, as shown in Fig.2. The signature peak of Ag2O nanocubes was observed at λ = 420 nm in samples Ag2O and Ag2O-PANI nanocubes, which is in good agreement with the previous reports [25,39]. Emeraldine PANI shows peaks at λ = 390 and 710 nm while Ag2O-PANI nanocubes show peaks at λ = 350, 420 and 780 nm. The peak at λ = 350 nm indicates the benzenoid π- π* transitions, whereas the peak at 780 nm indicates the benzenoid -quinoid ring transition of the polyaniline chain [42]. The blue shift in the peak corresponding to benzenoid π- π* transitions and the redshift in the peak corresponding to benzenoid-quinoid ring transition were observed for hybrid Ag2O-PANI which confirms the interaction between Ag2O and PANI. The absorption bands of green Ag2O-PANI observed at 350 nm, 420 nm and 780 nm vary according to the variation in pH from acidic to basic medium. According to previous reports, emeraldine PANI exhibits stability in an acidic medium (between pH = 2–4) whereas the absorbance intensity decreases gradually as the medium becomes alkaline [42]. Therefore, the pH-dependent stability experiment was conducted for Ag2O-PANI in the range of pH 2–10 as shown in Fig.S1. The color changed from green to blue as the pH changed from acidic to basic (pH 10) which indicates the conversion of PANI from salt to base form (non-conducting) as observed in previous reports [48,49]. Moreover, to understand the impact of the PANI on the stability of Ag2O nanocubes, we compared the absorbance spectra of pristine Ag2O nanocubes and Ag2O-PANI nanocubes after 60 days of their synthesis as shown in Fig.S2. We observed that the pristine Ag2O nanocubes had negligible absorption at 420 nm while Ag2O-PANI nanocubes showed prominent absorption bands at 350 nm, 420 nm and 780 nm. This indicates that the PANI enhances the stability of Ag2O nanocubes for a longer period, even when dispersed in DI water.

The morphology of as-synthesized Ag2O nanocubes was studied by TEM as shown in Fig.3.
Fig.3a depicts the heterogeneous distribution of Ag2O nanocubes. The size distribution was also calculated for 122 nanocubes. Maximum distribution of nanocubes (about 46%) was found in the range of 200–300 nm as shown in Fig.S3. Fig.3b shows the selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern of the Ag2O nanocubes which reveals the presence of (111) facet corresponding to the cuboctahedral shape of Ag2O nanocubes [50]. Fig.3c shows the High-Resolution TEM (HR-TEM) image of the as-prepared Ag2O nanocubes. The HR-TEM displays the lattice fringe with d-spacing of 0.26 nm corresponding to (100) plane of Ag2O [50].

The XRD was performed to study the crystallinity of the as-prepared Ag2O nanocubes and Ag2O-PANI nanocubes, as shown in Fig.4 , respectively. Five prominent diffraction peaks were observed at (111), (200), (220), (311) and (222) for both nanomaterials which are in good agreement with previous reports [39,51]. The diffraction peaks of Ag2O-PANI nanocubes are more intense and broader as compared to the diffraction peaks of pristine Ag2O nanocubes, as shown in Table S2. Table S2 It represents the full width half maximum (FWHM) data for Ag2O nanocubes and Ag2O-PANI nanocubes. This concludes that the PANI prohibits the agglomeration of Ag2O. We also observed the strong diffraction peaks corresponding to (111) and (200) planes indicating the cuboctahedra shape is consistent with the SAED pattern [39].

The molecular structures of PANI coated Ag2O nanocubes, PANI and Ag2O nanocubes were examined by using FTIR spectroscopy (Fig.5 ). The characteristic vibrational band of Ag2O was observed at 593.44 cm−1 in Ag2O-PANI and Ag2O nanocubes; this band generally occurs due to the transverse and longitudinal optical phonon vibrational frequency (this band generally appears in 500–600 cm−1 region but several reports also observed a distinct peak around 720 cm−1) [52–55]. There were several common peaks occurred in 1000–1800 cm−1 range corresponding to sulfonate groups attached to aromatic rings, C=C stretching of aromatic rings, CN stretching in benzenoid and quinoid units and vibration of OH band [56]. The peaks at 1398.35 and 1444.22 cm−1 shows the presence of CN stretching and C=C stretching of aromatic rings, respectively, which confirms the presence of PANI [57,58]. As we observed from the IR spectra of Ag2O-PANI nanocubes, the intensity of most of the peaks were reduced as compared to the pristine Ag2O which indicates the successful capping of PANI onto the Ag2O nanocubes.

Raman Spectra were observed to study the lattice vibrations of Ag2O, Ag2O-PANI and PANI as shown in Fig.6. There were several common peaks for Ag2O and Ag2O-PANI at ν (cm−1) = 293.6, 411.59, 473.25 and 687.74. The peak at 293.6 cm−1corresponds to Ag-O2vibrations due to chemisorbed molecular oxygen species on defects [59]. The peaks at 411.59 cm−1and 473.25 cm−1with high enhancement were observed after the coating of PANI that can be assigned to Ag2O bulk stretching vibrations, and the peak at 687.74 cm−1appeared due to silver nanoparticles which tends to come on to the surface from the crystal after the exposure of laser. This phenomenon has been observed before in various silver oxides or silver-based perovskites [60]. The peaks at 902.22 cm−1and 996.05 cm−1correspond to chemisorbed atomic/molecular oxygen species OO [60], and the peak at 1060 cm−1 is attributed to Ag-O stretch / vibrational mode. Several other peaks were observed corresponding to PANI at ν (cm−1) = 585.86, 765.4, 829.83, 945.11 and 1017.50 in Ag2O-PANI nanocube samples. However, the peaks of Raman spectra for PANI were very weak; only two peaks at 411.59 cm−1and 585.86 59 cm−1were significant. It can be concluded that the Ag2O enhanced the intensity of Raman peaks of PANI- Ag2O, which has been reported as a SERS substrate previously [61–63]. The peaks at 585.86 cm−1and 765.4 cm−1show the presence of phenoxazine –type units and deformation in Q ring, respectively. The peaks at 829.83 cm−1and 945.11 cm−1were due to B ring deformation whereas the peak at 1017.5 cm−1was corresponding to HSO4 −counter ion or SO3 −group in the sulfonated aromatic ring [64].

Pure quarternary structured hHb, shows electron bands at 279 (phenyl group of Trp-and Tyr), 349 (Ɛ band), 406 (soret or Heme band), 540 and 560 (oxy band). According to previous reports, silver can form a bioconjugate with Hb resulting in the redshift of soret band as the concentration of Hb increases leading to conformational changes [65]. The oxygen present in Ag2O further enhances the oxidation of Hb, and an electron transfer can be observed from Heme to Ag (Ag+ → Ag0). The electron transfer takes place due to the difference in the reduction potential of Ag+ (−1.8 V/NHE) and heme-Hb (+1.106 V/NHE) resulting in perturbation of the chemical environment of heme group suggesting the protein-silver interaction [66]. These results suggest that the absorption band at 410 nm does not get completely destructed but rather gets enhanced due to the displacement in the protein due to its interaction with the nanomaterial indicating a conformational change from its native form [67]. The mechanism can be explained as a distortion of the iron atom from the heme-porphyrin structure as the Ag2O-PANI nanocubes sits on the hydrophobic pocket of Hb. The oxidation of Hb leads to rupture of ionic and hydrogen bonds, which further leads to change in the movement of the polypeptide chain. This change in the movement can be noted as the soret redshift can be explained in terms of Gaussian shape, as explained by Boffi et al. [68]. The soret band can be portrayed as the convolution of three terms
A
( ν )
= L
( ν )
x G
( ν )
x P
( ν )

Where L(ν) represents the Lorentzian line shape accounting for absorption band of coupling of electron transition with high-frequency mode, G(ν) represents the Gaussian line broadening accounting for of electron transition with low frequency mode and P(ν) represents the spectral linewidth when both modes are taken into consideration giving rise to change in conformational heterogeneity. Boffi et al. emphasized that the soret band of the heme-porphyrin system delineates the Gaussian line, i.e. if iron atom present in the heme-porphyrin pocket moves out of heme-plane, it results in non-gaussian distribution. Fig.7
a and Fig.S4 vividly displays the spectral transition from Gaussian to a non-gaussian soret band indicating the out of plane orientation of iron atom present in the heme-porphyrin system. The UV–Vis spectra of Ɛ and oxy band near 349 nm and 540 nm respectively shows sequential increment in the absorption intensity indicating accessible nature of silver oxide resulting in an electronic transition. The behavioural pattern of Hb with PANI was also studied, which reflected the catalytic property of Hb towards PANI (Fig.S5)[69]. Plot 1/Hb vs 1/A-A0 confirmed the same behavioural pattern with linear relation (R2 = 0.96116) in correlation with the concentration of hHb (Fig.7b). Also time dependent studies were performed to confirm the increase in absorption intensity was due to increase in the concentration of Hb and not based on time of interaction between the nanomaterial and Hb (Fig.S6).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of Hb from whole blood in urine samples
ce:section.ce:para: Hematuria evaluation was performed by introducing Ag2O-PANI with varied concentration of RBCs (0.01 M–50 µM) extracted from a drop of finger prick blood sample from a healthy human volunteer, which was collected into a fresh, healthy urine sample. All experiments were performed after informed consent from donors and approval from the ethical committee of National Sun Yat-sen University, Taiwan of 2018–2019.

We observed sequential increment in the absorption of soret band due to the silver-protein bioconjugate as explained above. We obtained a limit of detection of 5.35 × 10−13 M with standard error of 0.087. As indicated in Table 1 , the 3 sets of replicated values obtained for human urine and human blood samples were highly identical with the spiked values, and the recoveries were obtained from 96.6% to 101.2%, which validated the reliability and practicality of this nanomaterial. Also, the pH value of urine was taken into consideration with respect to the stability of the nanocubes in different pH. Normal pH of human urine varies from 4.5 to 8, with an average of 6–7 [70]. The stability of Ag2O-PANI nanocubes changes after pH 8 (Fig.S1) and hence the nanomaterial is suitable to detect Hb in urine.

The anti-interference ability of Ag2O-PANI towards the detection of Hb was investigated by comparing it with major interfering sugars such as fructose, glucose, maltose along with amino acids include cysteine, arginine, lysine, asparagine, leucine and histidine (Fig.8). The concentration of Hb was 0.01 µM while all others at 10 µM. There was a significant increase in absorption intensity upon addition of Hb to Ag2O-PANI when compared to other components present in urine and blood (Fig.S7). Glucose shows a 2 fold increase in absorbance with the material as compared to fructose and maltose due to its affinity with PANI [69]. The absorption level of histidine and ferritin also indicates the interaction of the nanomaterial with the hydrophobic pocket of heme rather than proximal histidine-heme interactions. These results confirm good specificity and sensing method for the detection of hemoglobin in urine.

We report a sensitive and selective optical sensing method for hematuria analysis by Ag2O nanocubes stabilized with PANI. The stability and interaction of the nanomaterial was confirmed by UV–Vis Spectroscopy, FTIR, Raman, and XRD helped to understand the electron transition between Ag2O and Hb recording an absorption variation in a linear range of 0.005 µM to 0.05 µM with the limit of detection of 5.35 × 10−13 M. The nanomaterial presented was successfully applied to detect Hb from human whole blood in human urine samples. We believe that the current approach is an effective platform for future biomedicine analysis regarding human diseases for directly detecting Hb from human urine.
The author(s) declare that they have no competing interest.

All persons who meet authorship criteria are listed as authors, and all authors certify that they have participated sufficiently in the work to take public responsibility for the content, including participation in the concept, design, analysis, writing, or revision of the manuscript. Furthermore, each author certifies that this material or similar material has not been and will not be submitted to or published in any other publication before its appearance in the Microchemical Journal.

Nandini Swaminathan: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Visualization, Writing - original draft. Yowan Nerthigan: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing - review & editing. Hui-Fen Wu: Funding acquisition, Investigation, Methodology, Project administration, Supervision, Resources, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We thank the Ministry of Science and Technology (MOST) of Taiwan for financial support with the grant number of MOST 107-2113-M-110-011-MY3 is gratefully acknowledged.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.microc.2020.104723.
